1:
Paul shifts rhetorical mode without announcement. The Greek literary form called diatribē (diatribe) was a classroom debate technique used by Stoic and Cynic philosophers — the teacher addresses an imaginary interlocutor (often introduced by "O man" or "you there") and voices objections that the students would naturally raise, then answers them. Paul deploys this technique throughout chs. 2-3. The identity of the imaginary judge in 2:1 is initially left open — Gentile moralist? Jewish observer? — but becomes progressively clear as a Torah-confident Jew by 2:17.

The logic is a trap: the reader who nodded approvingly through ch. 1's indictment of Gentile depravity now finds themselves standing in the dock. "At whatever point you judge another, you are condemning yourself" — the structure of self-condemnation was already present in the prophetic tradition (cf. Nathan's parable to David, 2 Sam 12:1-7: "You are the man!").
2: "God's judgment is based on truth" (kata alētheian) — judicial impartiality was a prized ideal in ancient justice systems, though honored more in theory than practice. Roman courts were notoriously susceptible to patronage and influence. The claim that God judges "according to truth" is a counter-claim against the systems of social protection that insulated elites from accountability. The Jewish scriptures repeatedly insist on God's impartiality (Deut 10:17; 2 Chr 19:7; Job 34:19).
3: The diatribal style continues with a rhetorical question: the imaginary moralist thinks possession of the law or of correct judgment insulates them from divine accountability. This was a genuine Second Temple Jewish temptation — assuming that knowing the right thing and condemning the wrong thing was itself a form of righteousness. Paul counters: God's knowledge is not limited to public behavior but extends to the inner life and private practice ("you who pass judgment do the same things").
4: "The riches of his kindness, forbearance and patience" — three terms clustering around God's patience: chrēstotēs (goodness/kindness), anochē (forbearance, the suspension of deserved punishment), and makrothymia (long-suffering, patience with provocation). In Second Temple Jewish theology, God's delay of judgment was interpreted as a gift of time for repentance, not as evidence that sin did not matter (cf. 2 Pet 3:9; Ecclus 5:4-7 warns against presuming on God's mercy). Paul is correcting a specific misreading: God's patience is not approval.
5: "Storing up wrath against yourself for the day of God's wrath" — the metaphor of accumulation draws on the wisdom tradition (Prov 3:34 LXX; Sirach 3:28) and reverses the normal desire to accumulate wealth and honor. Instead of storing up treasure, the unrepentant accumulate condemnation. "The day of God's wrath" is eschatological — the final judgment — but Paul presents it as the logical culmination of a process already underway. The "day of judgment" language is widespread in Second Temple literature (1 Enoch 22; 4 Ezra 7).
6: The quotation is from Ps 62:12 (LXX 61:13) — "He will repay each person according to their deeds" — a foundational principle that Paul deploys here to establish the universal standard before he applies it asymmetrically. This text was cited in early Jewish contexts (Prov 24:12; Job 34:11; Sirach 35:22) and was sufficiently common that Paul can introduce it simply with "it is written" — the standard citation formula for authoritative scripture.
7: The description of those who will receive eternal life — "those who by persistence in doing good seek glory, honor and immortality" — uses the vocabulary of the Greco-Roman honor quest. "Glory, honor, and immortality" were the supreme objects of elite Roman ambition, pursued through military achievement, political office, and memory preserved in monuments. Paul does not reject these aspirations but redirects their object: seeking them from God rather than human society. The reorientation of the honor system underlies much of Paul's ethical teaching.
8: "Self-seeking" (eritheiai) — the word originally described the behavior of a hired political worker who would work for any faction for pay, thus connoting factional self-interest over common good. By Paul's time it had acquired the sense of selfish ambition or contentiousness. "Reject the truth" and "follow evil" present the same exchange pattern as 1:25 — the willful substitution of falsehood for truth in the sphere of action.
9: "Trouble and distress for every human being who does evil: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile" — the ordering of "first for the Jew" in judgment echoes the "first for the Jew" in salvation (1:16). Greater privilege entails greater accountability, a principle rooted in the prophetic tradition (Amos 3:2: "You only have I chosen of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your sins"). Jewish election was never meant to be a shield against judgment but an intensification of moral responsibility.
10: "But glory, honor and peace for everyone who does good: first for the Jew, then for the Gentile" — the chiastic parallel with v. 9 confirms the structural symmetry: the same God who judges impartially also rewards impartially. "Peace" (eirēnē) here carries its full Hebrew shalom weight — comprehensive well-being and covenantal wholeness. The repetition of "first for the Jew" in both directions (blessing and judgment) maintains the covenant-historical priority while denying it as a protection from accountability.
11: "God does not show favoritism" (ou gar estin prosōpolēmpsia para tō theō) — prosōpolēmpsia (lit., "face-taking") translates the Hebrew nasa panim (lifting the face, i.e., showing partiality). The principle was embedded in Torah (Lev 19:15; Deut 1:17; 10:17) and developed extensively in Jewish ethical teaching. For Paul, it is not just a judicial principle but the theological ground for the universal scope of both judgment and salvation. A partial God would be a tribal God; the God of Israel is the God of all.
12: Paul introduces a crucial distinction: Gentiles who sin will be judged "apart from the law" (anomōs) — by a standard they were never given. Jews who sin will be judged "by the law" — by the standard they received and claimed to honor. This is not a two-tiered justice system but a single standard of accountability calibrated to knowledge. The principle was recognized in Second Temple ethics: "those who sin without knowledge sin without guilt" (cf. Num 15:22-31, the distinction between inadvertent and deliberate sin).
13: "It is not those who hear the law who are righteous in God's sight, but those who obey the law who will be declared righteous" — Paul counters a specific Second Temple Jewish assumption. In some streams of Second Temple thought, possession and study of the Torah was itself meritorious (cf. Sirach 24; Baruch 4:1). Paul insists the Torah is not a badge of status but a demand for performance. Hearing (akroatai) vs. doing (poiētai) — the distinction between the passive receipt and active obedience of law — echoes James 1:22-25 and the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 7:24-27).
14: The concept of Gentiles doing "by nature" (physei) what the law requires touches on Stoic natural law philosophy. The Stoics taught that a universal rational principle (logos) was inscribed in the cosmos and in human reason, accessible to all people regardless of their particular cultural tradition. Paul appropriates this concept: Gentiles who behave morally demonstrate that something morally ordering is at work in them, even without the Mosaic law. This is not a claim that Gentiles are naturally good but that they have access to a moral standard even apart from Torah.
15: "They show that the requirements of the law are written on their hearts" — the language echoes Jer 31:33, the new covenant promise of the law written on the heart. Paul is not saying Gentile moral behavior fulfills the new covenant; he is arguing more narrowly that it proves moral knowledge is available outside the Torah. "Conscience" (syneidēsis) was a significant term in Stoic moral philosophy — the inner witness that knows one's own actions for what they are. Seneca used it (roughly contemporary with Paul) to describe the interior judge that cannot be bribed. Paul adopts the Greek term but grounds it in creation rather than pure reason.
16: "This will take place on the day when God judges people's secrets through Jesus Christ, as my gospel declares" — the day of judgment is identified as the framework within which the present argument operates. "People's secrets" (ta krypta tōn anthrōpōn) — God's judgment reaches what human courts cannot: the inner life of motive, intention, and private action. The phrase "as my gospel declares" is a striking insertion — Paul claims that the gospel is not only about salvation but also about judgment, and that Jesus is the agent of both.
17: Having established the universal moral standard, Paul now turns to the Jewish reader directly. The diatribe intensifies: "if you call yourself a Jew" (ei de su Ioudaios eponomazē). The name "Jew" (from Judah) had become in the diaspora a term of religious identity and self-description, carrying enormous pride. "Rely on the law and boast in God" — these were genuine marks of Jewish identity. The Torah was Israel's distinctive gift (Deut 4:8: "What other nation is so great as to have such righteous decrees and laws?"). Paul does not deny the gift; he challenges the assumption that receipt of the gift confers status before God.
18: "Approve of what is superior" (dokimazeis ta diapheronta) — "test and approve the things that excel/differ." This refers to the capacity for moral discernment cultivated through Torah study and practice. In the synagogue tradition, the ability to distinguish the clean from the unclean, the permitted from the forbidden, the righteous from the wicked, was the mark of the learned teacher. Paul acknowledges this capacity; the question is whether it has issued in the corresponding practice.
19: "Convinced that you are a guide for the blind, a light for those in the dark" — the imagery is drawn from Israel's election as a light to the nations (Isa 42:6-7; 49:6). Isaiah describes the servant of the Lord as "a light for the Gentiles, to open eyes that are blind, to free captives from prison." The Jewish reader Paul addresses had applied this calling to themselves: Israel as the teaching people who would bring the Gentiles to knowledge of God. Paul does not dispute the calling; he questions whether those who claimed it lived up to it.
20: "The embodiment of knowledge and truth" (tēn morphōsin tēs gnōseōs kai tēs alētheias en tō nomō) — the Torah is "the form/embodiment" of knowledge and truth. Morphōsis can mean either "form" (the outward shape) or "embodiment" (the actual instantiation). The ambiguity is probably deliberate: Paul's critique will be that the Jewish teacher has the form without the substance, the shape without the reality.
21: Paul's rhetorical questions in vv. 21-23 follow the pattern of prophetic indictment. Amos, Isaiah, and Jeremiah repeatedly confronted Israel with the gap between their professed covenant loyalty and their actual behavior. The specific sins mentioned (stealing, adultery, temple robbery) are not random; they represent different categories of law: civil, sexual, and religious. The point is not that every Jewish teacher committed these acts but that the pattern of inconsistency was evident enough to be a live charge.
22: "You who abhor idols, do you rob temples?" — hierosyleō (rob temples) could refer to looting pagan temples (which Jewish sources sometimes condemned) or to the misappropriation of temple funds within Jewish religious practice. In the diaspora, Jewish communities sometimes depended on temple treasuries for communal welfare; misuse of these funds would be a form of sacrilege. The charge is not just hypocrisy in general but the specific irony of professing horror at idolatry while committing what amounted to desecration of their own sacred institutions.
23: "Do you dishonor God by breaking the law?" — the charge ties together the entire section: the Jew who boasts in the Torah (v. 17, 23) but breaks it dishonors the God whose name the Torah invokes. The indictment is not about Jewish law-observance being irrelevant but about the misuse of law as a source of ethnic pride rather than as a call to covenant faithfulness.
24: The quotation is from Isa 52:5 (LXX): "God's name is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you." The original context in Isaiah is the humiliation of Israel in exile — the nations, seeing Israel's defeat, concluded that Israel's God was weaker than the Babylonian gods. Paul applies the verse to the diaspora Jewish community whose behavior in the Roman world was creating a negative reputation for God. The logic is uncomfortable: Jewish misconduct in Rome was, in Paul's reading, a form of blasphemy against the God of Israel.
25:
Circumcision was the foundational physical marker of Jewish covenant membership (Gen 17:10-14). It was required of all male Jews and of all male converts (gerim) to Judaism. In the second-temple period it was the most visible and controversial mark distinguishing Jews from Gentiles; Antiochus IV had prohibited it (1 Macc 1:48), and Jewish mothers had been martyred for circumcising their sons (1 Macc 1:60-61). The diaspora Jewish community defended circumcision intensely against Hellenistic pressure to abandon it.

Paul does not abolish circumcision's covenantal significance; he insists on its conditional character. The Torah itself made this argument: Lev 26:41 speaks of "uncircumcised hearts" among circumcised Israelites, and Deut 10:16 commands Israel to "circumcise the foreskin of your heart." Circumcision was always a sign of a prior covenant reality, not the reality itself.
26: "If those who are not circumcised keep the law's requirements, will they not be regarded as though they were circumcised?" — the counterfactual is deliberately provocative. Paul is not describing a common social reality (observant uncircumcised Gentiles were rare) but making a logical point: if the covenant requirement is moral performance, then covenant status follows performance, not the physical sign. This anticipates the Abraham argument in ch. 4, where Paul shows that Abraham was justified before circumcision (Gen 15:6 precedes Gen 17).
27: "The one who is not circumcised physically and yet obeys the law will condemn you" — the language of judgment is striking: a Gentile's obedience becomes juridical evidence against the disobedient Jew. This is not a claim that Gentile obedience is common or normative but a logical argument. The same rhetorical move appears in Matt 12:41-42, where Nineveh and the Queen of the South "condemn" Jesus's generation at the judgment — their faithful response becomes a witness against unfaithfulness.
28: "A person is not a Jew who is one only outwardly" (en tō phanerō, lit. "in what is visible/manifest") — Paul draws on a tradition within Jewish thought that distinguished between ethnic and spiritual Israel. The prophets had warned that physical descent from Abraham was no guarantee of covenant standing (Jer 9:26 condemns "all who are circumcised only in the flesh"; Ezek 44:7 speaks of those "uncircumcised in heart"). Paul is not inventing a new distinction but applying a prophetic one.
29:
"Circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code" — the phrase "circumcision of the heart" draws on Deut 30:6 ("The Lord your God will circumcise your hearts and the hearts of your descendants") and Jer 4:4 ("Circumcise yourselves to the Lord, circumcise your hearts"). Paul identifies the "Spirit" as the agent of this inner transformation, placing the new covenant fulfillment of Deut 30:6 within the present experience of the gospel.

"Such a person's praise is not from other people, but from God" — epainos (praise) plays on the etymology of the name Judah (Yehudah), derived from the Hebrew root yadah (to praise, to give thanks). A true Jew is one whose praise comes from God — a wordplay that only those familiar with the Hebrew tradition would catch.
